The note uses "the agent needs to know," "the agent needs to reason about," and "the agent either knows what structured-claim means." These attribute knowledge and reasoning to the agent, but in the context of this KB — where "agent" refers to an LLM operating in a tool loop — "knows" and "needs to reason about" are reasonable shorthand for "has in context" and "must process." The note itself clarifies this: "must be in the context window." No misleading anthropomorphism detected.

The note claims "The types that genuinely move are text and note" and that everything else is directory-local. But `structured-claim` is explicitly designed to work across directories. The note's own linked source (document-types-should-be-verifiable.md) says traits and types are directory-independent, and the orthogonality note (referenced in this note's "What moves between directories?" section) argued that "a structured-claim works identically in notes/, claw-design/, or anywhere." The note acknowledges this counter-argument but only as a quote it then dismisses with "But in practice, how many documents actually move?" This is a rhetorical dismissal rather than a refutation. If structured-claim is genuinely portable (which the note concedes), then the claim that only text and note are portable is false by the note's own admission. The open questions section partially acknowledges this ("Does structured-claim stay global?") but the body's assertion is stated categorically.

The note cites why-directories-despite-their-costs.md as supporting the idea that "directories already carry local conventions; this note proposes making that load-bearing for types." However, the directories note contains an explicit section titled "Types and directories are orthogonal" which directly argues the opposite: "If types depended on directories, you'd need to redefine types whenever someone creates a new subdirectory." and "The document classification system should work across any directory structure." The cited source actively opposes the proposal this note makes. The note is borrowing the directories note's observation about local conventions while ignoring its explicit argument that types should NOT be directory-scoped. This is selective attribution.

check: Grounding — claim count mismatch with document-classification.md
finding: The note states "the document classification spec defines seven global base types: text, note, structured-claim, spec, review, index, adr." The actual document-classification.md lists exactly seven rows in its base types table, so the count is correct. However, the note frames these as types that are all "loaded every session whether relevant or not." But document-classification.md itself has already been updated to acknowledge directory-scoped types: "The type field is a free-form string. The table below lists the common values; directory-scoped types/ folders document the structural expectations for each." The source already accommodates directory-local definitions — the note frames the source as more rigid than it currently is.

The note proposes that `index` is a "directory-local specialisation of note." But indexes are cross-cutting navigation artifacts. A tags-index.md or links-index.md serves the entire KB, not a single directory. Indexes don't belong to any one directory the way an ADR belongs to adr/. The note's framework doesn't clearly account for types that are structurally specialized but not directory-local in their scope of service.

check: Completeness — boundary case: on-demand loading already exists
finding: The note frames the situation as a binary: either types are loaded globally upfront or they are directory-scoped. The note itself acknowledges a third option — "If we had real on-demand type resolution... types could be global names with on-demand definitions, just like programming" — but treats it as hypothetical. In practice, the CLAUDE.md routing table + escalation boundaries already implement partial on-demand resolution: the agent sees type names in the routing table and loads type definitions only when about to write one. This is closer to the "import" mechanism than the note acknowledges, which weakens the framing that directory-scoping is the only available workaround.

check: Internal consistency — tension between "current implementation" and body argument
finding: The "Current implementation" section claims the proposal is already implemented: "The CLAUDE.md routing table and content workflow now implement this proposal." But the body argues for reducing the global type vocabulary to just text and note, while document-classification.md still lists seven base types and CLAUDE.md's routing table still references `structured-claim` as a type. The implementation section describes progressive disclosure (load specialized types on demand), which is a weaker claim than the body's proposal (eliminate most global types). The note quietly shifts from "shrink the global vocabulary" to "load definitions on demand" without marking this as a different proposal.

check: Internal consistency — "What stays global" list vs. body
finding: The "What stays global" section lists "Status ladder — seedling/current/speculative/outdated" as a global affordance. But the body's thin global layer table lists only `text` and `note` as global types, and the status ladder is a property of `note`, not a separate type. This is not a contradiction, but the note conflates two different meanings of "global": global types (the type field vocabulary) and global conventions (properties that all notes share). The "What stays global" section is about conventions, not types, but this distinction is never made explicit.

The "Where affordances actually live" table accurately maps structural expectations to their actual locations. Cross-checking: related-systems, adr, tasks, sources, and structured-claim structural requirements are indeed defined in directory-local READMEs/templates, not in the global type field. The observation that the global type field mostly says `note` for these is accurate.

The note cites this as establishing "the loading hierarchy: always-loaded surfaces should be slim, task-specific detail loads on demand." The source confirms this: "match instruction specificity to loading frequency. Universal rules load always. Task-specific rules load when doing that task." The attribution is accurate and the economic argument for types correctly extends this principle to the type system domain.

The note cites ADR-002 as the decision to inline the note template in WRITING.md. ADR-002 confirms: "Inline the note and structured-claim templates directly into kb/instructions/WRITING.md." The attribution is accurate.

The note cites a-functioning-kb-needs-a-workshop-layer-not-just-a-library.md for the claim that "workshop subsystems (tasks, queues) already define their own types locally." The source confirms: "Each temporal subsystem (tasks, decision threads, experiments, queues) has its own state machine... local conventions per subsystem... is likely the right default." The attribution is accurate.

The programming section's argument is internally coherent: it identifies that the cost difference between programming types and LLM types comes from the resolution mechanism (compiler import vs. context pre-loading), and correctly identifies directory-scoping as a workaround for the lack of an import mechanism. The analogy holds without contradiction.

The open questions section honestly surfaces tensions that the body does not fully resolve (structured-claim's status, the type field's future, searchability). This is appropriate for a seedling-status note.

The description correctly hedges with "Proposes that discovery has a dual structure," but the body asserts the framework as established fact: "has a dual structure that can't be decomposed," "The general doesn't exist until you see the particular as an instance of it," "They co-arise." The three-depth hierarchy is likewise asserted — "Similarity-based connections vary not by *kind* (topical vs mechanistic) but by *depth*" — with no hedging that this is the note's own proposed framework. The note's status is "seedling," which further widens the gap between epistemic status and prose confidence.

The title claim — discovery's co-arising dual structure — receives roughly two paragraphs of development (the opening section). The "Three depths of abstraction" section (table + surrounding prose) and "Recognition is the hard problem" section each receive comparable or greater treatment. The depth hierarchy and recognition-cost argument are valuable, but they extend the core claim into knowledge-system design rather than grounding the core claim itself. A reader looking for the argument that discovery has this dual structure finds a brief assertion and two examples, then the note pivots to a different (related) framework.

The opening claims broad scope — "whether in mathematics, science, or a knowledge base." The "Recognition is the hard problem" section narrows without signposting to knowledge-system-specific terms: "embeddings, keywords, filenames," "linking two notes," "creating a new note that names the mechanism." This is consistent with the description's scope ("Proposes... and that similarity-based connections vary by abstraction depth") but a reader coming through the title would expect the recognition discussion to stay domain-general. The shift is not a defect — the KB-specific application is where the note's practical value lives — but a transitional sentence flagging the narrowing would help.

The note claims the creative act is "the same" across mathematics (lemma extraction), biology (Darwin), and knowledge bases — "the creative act is the same: recognizing that things from different contexts are instances of a structure that nobody had named yet." This is a strong transfer claim. The examples are illustrative rather than evidential, which is fine, but the word "same" asserts structural identity across domains without stating what shared mechanism makes the transfer valid. A reader could reasonably ask: is lemma extraction (formal deduction) really the same operation as Darwin's synthesis (empirical induction)?

The three-depth table uses natural-language labels (Shared feature / Shared structure / Generative model) and plain descriptions of power levels. No mathematical notation or variable apparatus. The table genuinely organizes distinct concepts rather than decorating a verbal argument. Clean.

Historical examples (Darwin, phlogiston, caloric theory, Fleming) are well-known and used illustratively, not as empirical evidence requiring citation. No specific numbers, percentages, or named studies appear without context. The Alexander reference is linked to its source note. Clean.

Each section opens with new material. "Three depths of abstraction" introduces the hierarchy without restating the dual-structure claim. "Recognition is the hard problem" opens with a new distinction (linking vs. recognition) rather than recapping the hierarchy. No inter-section restatement detected. Clean.
The note addresses discovery as a human cognitive act ("You posit," "you recognize") and does not attribute mental states to models. No LLM-directed anthropomorphism present. Clean.

The three-depths hierarchy (shared feature / shared structure / generative model) claims to be a depth ordering where "each level subsumes the previous." Boundary case: **analogical reasoning** — recognizing that electrical circuits and water pipes share relational structure (Gentner's structure mapping) is deeper than shared features but does not propose a generative model that *produces* both phenomena. It sits cleanly at "shared structure." However, **metaphor** — e.g., "argument is war" (Lakoff) — maps structure across domains in a way that shapes reasoning and generates predictions, yet does not propose a causal mechanism. It falls between shared structure and generative model: it has generative power (produces new inferences) but is not an "abstract machine that produces both phenomena." The hierarchy's middle level ("extract a common pattern") and top level ("propose an abstract machine") leave a gap for mappings that generate inferences without positing a mechanism. The note's own phrasing — "Explanatory — explains why the similarity exists and predicts new instances" — bundles explanation and prediction together, but productive metaphors predict without explaining.

The note claims the three-depths hierarchy "draws on Alexander's levels of concreteness — from structural templates through generative processes to mutual reinforcement." The Alexander note describes three levels of a different kind: (1) patterns as document types (concrete structure), (2) generative processes vs master plans (emergent methodology), (3) centers strengthening centers (mutual reinforcement). These are levels of *architectural abstraction* in knowledge system design, not levels of *similarity depth*. The note maps them as: structural templates -> shared structure, generative processes -> generative model. But Alexander's "generative processes" are about *how good structure emerges incrementally*, not about *proposing an abstract machine that produces two phenomena*. The reuse is creative but the attribution ("draws on") overstates the derivation. The hierarchy is the note's own construction; the Alexander connection is suggestive, not foundational.

The link to "information value is observer-relative" is annotated "grounds: the recognition cost hierarchy maps to computational bounds on structure extraction." The information-value note does discuss extraction cost and bounded observers, and it does reference back to this discovery note: "Discovery cost is observer-relative but not easily optimized — the data from which a connection could be inferred is present before anyone sees it, but extracting the pattern requires computation that scales with abstraction depth." However, the information-value note's framework is about *observer-relative value* (prior knowledge, computational capacity, tools, goals). The discovery note's recognition cost hierarchy is about *depth of abstraction* (surface vs structural vs generative). These are related but not the same: recognition cost could be observer-relative (a domain expert recognizes structural similarity cheaply), but the discovery note presents it as an intrinsic property of depth levels ("surface similarity: cheap... structural similarity: expensive"), not as observer-relative. The grounding claim is partially valid (the information-value note mentions this note's hierarchy) but the relationship is more accurately "extends" or "consistent with" than "grounds," since the information-value framework would actually complicate the discovery note's cost hierarchy by making it observer-dependent rather than depth-dependent.

The "dual structure" claim — that discovery requires simultaneously positing the general and recognizing the particular as an instance — is presented as indecomposable ("can't be decomposed"). Boundary case: **incremental generalization** where an observer first notices a recurring pattern across several instances (recognition accumulates) and only later names it as a concept (positing follows). The note's examples (mathematician extracting a lemma, Darwin) are compatible with the co-arising framing, but many real discoveries involve a gradual accumulation phase before the "aha" moment. The note could be describing the logical structure of the completed discovery rather than its temporal process. If so, the claim is defensible but should be read as structural, not processual.

The note explicitly scopes its hierarchy to "similarity-based connections" and names contrastive, causal, and temporal links as outside scope. Boundary case: **explanatory links** — "X explains Y" — can be similarity-based (if X and Y share generative structure) or causal (if X causes Y). The scope boundary between similarity and causation may be less clean than the parenthetical suggests, since the "generative model" level explicitly involves explaining "why the similarity exists," which borders on causal explanation.

The recognition cost hierarchy claims surface similarity is "cheap" because "embeddings, keywords, filenames get you there." Boundary case: **misleading surface similarity** — two notes that share vocabulary but discuss fundamentally different phenomena (e.g., "cell" in biology vs "cell" in spreadsheets). Surface tools will flag these as similar, producing false positives. The hierarchy addresses cost of recognition but not cost of *discrimination* — telling true similarity from coincidental vocabulary overlap. This is the flip side of the "adjacency is not connection" distinction from the arscontexta note, which the discovery note already links.

The note claims "the highest-value act isn't linking two notes that share a mechanism — it's creating a new note that names the mechanism." This is stated as a conclusion from the naming-amortizes-discovery-cost argument. But the argument only shows that naming reduces *future recognition cost*. It does not show that naming is higher value than linking, since linking also has value (connecting existing knowledge for traversal). The inference is plausible — naming has compounding returns while linking has local returns — but the note presents it as a direct consequence rather than an additional claim that needs its own support.

The link to the arscontexta note is annotated "refines: the 'controlled disorder' claim is right about judgment-based linking but the topic-vs-mechanism framing is a false dichotomy." The arscontexta note's "controlled disorder" claim (from Luhmann via article #23) is about requiring each cross-topical link to carry a defensible reason. The discovery note's refinement — that the relevant axis is depth, not topic-vs-mechanism — is a reasonable reinterpretation. However, the arscontexta note does not itself use a "topic-vs-mechanism" framing; it uses "adjacency-vs-connection." The "topic-vs-mechanism" framing may come from a different source or from the discovery note's own reading of the conceptual landscape. The refinement target is slightly misattributed.

The three-depths hierarchy is internally consistent. The table's "Operation" and "Power" columns align: naming a surface similarity is descriptive, extracting a common pattern is structural, proposing an abstract machine is explanatory. The subsumption claim holds: a generative model implies shared structure which implies shared features. No section contradicts another.

The "dual structure" framing in the opening is consistent with how discovery is used later: naming a mechanism (positing the general) and recognizing instances (seeing the particular). The concept is introduced and applied consistently throughout.

The link to "minimum viable vocabulary" is annotated "grounds: MVV reframes 'naming amortizes discovery cost' as an optimization problem." The MVV note explicitly states: "Naming amortizes discovery cost. Discovery argues that the highest-value act in a knowledge system is creating a named concept — once a structure has a name, recognizing further instances becomes cheap." This is a faithful attribution. The MVV note does build an optimization frame on top of the discovery note's naming claim. The grounding relationship is genuine and accurately described.

The link to the "Notes Without Reasons" ingest is annotated "extends: the adjacency-vs-connection distinction maps to recognition depth, not link kind." The ingest note confirms this connection explicitly in its connections section: "the adjacency-vs-connection distinction maps to this note's recognition depth hierarchy; embedding adjacency is 'shared feature' level, curated links require deeper recognition." Both notes agree on the mapping. The relationship is accurately described.

The note's explicit scope limitation — "This hierarchy covers similarity-based connections" with named exclusions (contrastive, causal, temporal) — is a genuine epistemic boundary, not a hedge. The note does not overreach into the excluded domains.

The title "Distillation status determines directory placement" asserts a principle, but the note explicitly self-identifies as a "hunch" (description field: "Hunch that...") and a proposal (opening paragraph: "This note proposes sharpening that boundary"). The title uses the assertive "determines" rather than a hedged form like "should determine" or "could determine." The body correctly hedges ("This note proposes," "Why this might work"), but the title reads as established principle. This is a mild case — seedling status and the description partially compensate — but the title is the primary retrieval surface and carries the strongest framing.

The note was triggered by the placement of WRITING.md, and the second paragraph is devoted to this specific case. The rest of the note generalizes well, but the WRITING.md example is used both as motivating instance and as confirming evidence ("Moving WRITING.md to instructions doesn't break workflows"). Since the note proposes a general organizational principle, having the confirming-evidence section reference the same artifact that triggered the note is slightly circular. This is not a hard failure — the example is legitimate — but worth flagging.

The core claim is that distillation status should be the directory boundary criterion. The section that carries the most weight for this claim is the opening paragraph plus "Why this might work" (3 bullet points, roughly 100 words). The "How to evaluate" section (confirming and disconfirming evidence, roughly 150 words) is longer. The evaluative apparatus outweighs the argument itself. For a seedling note this is reasonable — the evaluation criteria are the note's main contribution at this stage — but if the note matures, the argument section will need proportional development.
No formal notation, variables, or equations appear. The note uses natural language throughout. Clean.
No specific figures, data points, percentages, or named studies appear. All claims are conceptual and internally grounded. Clean.
The note operates entirely within the KB-design domain. No cross-domain evidence is cited. Clean.

Each section opens with new material. "Why this might work" introduces three distinct arguments. "How to evaluate" introduces confirming and disconfirming criteria. "Open Questions" raises three new issues. No section restates a prior section's conclusion. Clean.

The note refers to agents executing procedures and deliberating with reasoning, which are conventional terms in this KB's vocabulary (agent-as-tool-loop). No language attributes human cognitive states to models. Clean.

The note proposes a binary boundary (procedure -> instructions/, reasoning -> notes/) but the codebase contains type templates in kb/notes/types/ (structured-claim.md, adr.md, index.md, related-system.md) that are procedural artifacts -- skeleton templates with imperative guidance on how to fill sections -- living inside kb/notes/. These are not discursive reasoning; they are execution-oriented artifacts that tell an agent "put X here." The note's framework does not account for them. They are a clear case that falls between the two categories: procedural in function but serving as structural templates rather than standalone procedures. The note's own disconfirming-evidence section anticipates "genuinely mixed" artifacts, but type templates are not mixed -- they are unambiguously procedural, just a different kind of procedure (structural template vs. step-sequenced workflow). This is a gap in the framework, not a known limitation.

The note states WRITING.md "sits at the top of the KB directory as a writing guide." In the current filesystem, WRITING.md already lives at kb/instructions/WRITING.md, not at kb/. The note's "immediate trigger" -- that WRITING.md is misplaced and should move to instructions/ -- appears to describe a state that no longer holds (or never held in the current tree). This weakens the motivating example, since the artifact the note uses to illustrate the principle is already where the principle says it should be.

CLAUDE.md itself contains imperative instructions (git rules, conventions, routing tables) alongside discursive vocabulary definitions. By the note's criterion, the imperative portions are procedural and the discursive portions are reasoning. CLAUDE.md is a mixed artifact that the binary boundary cannot cleanly classify. The note might argue CLAUDE.md is a special case (control-plane router), but its existence as a prominent mixed artifact is worth noting as a boundary case the framework should address.

The note frames the boundary as "distilled into a procedure" vs. "discursive reasoning," but there is a third category the KB already uses: source snapshots and ingests (kb/sources/). These are neither procedures nor discursive reasoning -- they are reference material. The note's binary does not need to cover sources (they have their own directory), but the omission means the note is not proposing a complete organizational principle for the KB, only a sharper boundary between two of its directories. This is implicit but never stated explicitly.

The note claims the loading-frequency hierarchy aligns with the procedural/discursive split: "Procedures are loaded when an agent needs to act; discursive notes are loaded when an agent needs to reason." The linked note (instruction-specificity-should-match-loading-frequency.md) describes a hierarchy organized by specificity and loading frequency (CLAUDE.md -> skill descriptions -> skill bodies -> task-specific docs), not by reading mode. The alignment the note claims is a reasonable inference, but the source note does not frame it in terms of "reading mode" or "procedure vs. reasoning." The note is extending the source's framework rather than attributing it.

The note's third open question asks: "How does this interact with the constraining gradient? Distillation and constraining are different operations -- does the directory boundary conflate them?" This is an important self-identified tension. The linked note (methodology-enforcement-is-constraining.md) places instructions on a constraining gradient (ad hoc prompt -> instruction -> skill -> hook -> script). In that framework, instructions are defined by their position on the enforcement-reliability axis, not by their distillation status. The two organizational principles (distillation status vs. enforcement position) could produce conflicting placement recommendations for artifacts that are highly constrained but not procedurally distilled, or procedurally distilled but loosely enforced. The note flags this honestly as an open question, which is appropriate.

The link to skills-derive-from-methodology-through-distillation.md is well-grounded. That note defines distillation as the operation that transforms discursive reasoning into procedural instruction while preserving the same medium. The current note accurately uses this definition as the basis for its directory-boundary proposal. The attribution ("defines the distillation relationship") is precise.

The link to instructions-are-skills-without-automatic-routing.md is accurately characterized. That note establishes that instructions are "optimized for execution, not understanding" and belong in kb/instructions/ because "the directory tells the agent what kind of reading the document demands." The current note's proposal is a natural extension of this existing convention.

The parallel with areas-exist-because-useful-operations-require-reading-notes-together.md is reasonable. That note argues areas group notes for co-reading operations (orientation, comparative reading). The current note proposes grouping by maintenance operation (auditing procedures as a set). The parallel holds: both use an operation-driven rationale for directory grouping rather than a topic-driven one.

The note is internally consistent. The three "why this might work" arguments (maintenance, legibility, loading hierarchy) do not contradict each other. The confirming/disconfirming evidence section is well-constructed and honestly anticipates the main weakness (mixed artifacts). The open questions do not undermine the claims -- they identify genuine boundaries of the proposal.

The note's status as "seedling" and its framing as a "hunch" are appropriately calibrated. It does not overclaim -- it proposes a principle, offers reasons it might work, identifies what would disconfirm it, and lists open questions. The rhetorical posture matches the epistemic status.

"Distillation creates something new from something larger" and "Most KB learning is distillation" (section "The dominant mechanism in knowledge work") are asserted as fact, but these are the note's own framework claims, not established results. The note introduces a specific decomposition (distillation vs constraining as co-equal, orthogonal mechanisms) that is original to this KB, yet the language throughout treats it as settled — "one of two co-equal learning mechanisms," "constraining and distillation are orthogonal," "creation matters more than hardening." No hedging signals that this is a proposed model rather than a consensus position.

The core claim is that distillation is "compressing knowledge for a specific task under a context budget." The section that carries the most weight for that claim — "How distillation works" — is well-developed (~270 words of prose plus a table). However, "The dominant mechanism in knowledge work" (~90 words) makes the bold claim that distillation is the primary learning mechanism AND that "creation matters more than hardening," yet gives only a single-cycle sketch as support. This is the note's strongest and most consequential claim and it gets the thinnest treatment.

The "Prior work" section references Vygotsky's scaffolding, Bloom's taxonomy, Nonaka & Takeuchi's externalization, and professional abstracting/indexing. These are appropriate for establishing prior art, but "progressive disclosure is distillation applied to documentation" equates the two without qualification — progressive disclosure is an information architecture pattern about sequencing, not necessarily about compression under a budget. This is a minor conceptual stretch rather than domain leakage, but it could mislead readers familiar with progressive disclosure.

The opening paragraph defines distillation, then the second paragraph re-situates it relative to context engineering. The "Why distillation exists" section opens with "Different operational contexts need different things from the same body of knowledge" — which is a restatement of the opening paragraph's point that distillation targets "an artifact that equips a consumer (agent, collaborator) to perform a task." The section's own contribution (the agent statelessness argument) starts in its second paragraph. The restatement is mild — one sentence rather than a full paragraph — so this is informational rather than a warning.

The note's claimed generality level is agent-operated knowledge bases. The vocabulary throughout is consistent with that domain: "context budget," "agent," "artifact," "skill," "note," "workshop." No leaked vocabulary from a narrower origin domain (e.g., no software engineering debugging examples presented as general, no ML training vocabulary used outside the explicit terminology section). The prior-work survey is framed appropriately as prior art, not as the note's own domain.

The note contains two tables: a Source-to-Distillate mapping and a 2x2 constrained/distilled grid. Both do real work — the first concretizes the abstract definition with specific KB-relevant examples, the second makes the orthogonality claim precise by showing that all four quadrant combinations are populated. Neither could be removed without losing clarity. No variables, equations, or formal notation appear.

All specific references are adequately contextualized. "Hinton et al., 2015" is named and its meaning explained in the terminology section. Vygotsky, Bloom, and Nonaka & Takeuchi are named with enough context to identify the relevant concepts. The "Faithful Self-Evolvers" paper is cited with a link and its finding is summarized. No floating numbers, percentages, or unsourced empirical claims.

The prior-work section cites pedagogy, technical writing, library science, and knowledge management — but frames them as analogues ("is not new — it's the core of several established fields"), not as evidence that distillation works in the agent context. The concluding paragraph of that section explicitly states what's specific to the agent context. The ML distillation comparison in the terminology section explicitly bridges the domains and notes the divergence. No unbridged transfers found.

The note avoids attributing mental states to models. Agents are described as "performing," "needing," "facing" tasks — operational language, not cognitive. "The model" does not appear as a subject with mental-state verbs. The closest case is "a collaborator who needs the current strategic picture," but "collaborator" here refers to a human or agent in a functional role, not to a model's internal state.

Reorganization without volume reduction (reordering, splitting, linking) sits ambiguously in the 2x2 matrix -- it changes extractable value without clearly being distillation or constraining. The note could clarify whether "compression" includes restructuring that increases density-per-task even without reducing total volume.

Deletion/pruning and schema evolution are learning-relevant operations that don't map to either mechanism, suggesting the "two co-equal mechanisms" framing may undercount or needs explicit scoping to content-level operations.

The link to epiplexity claims it "measures theoretically what distillation does operationally," but epiplexity covers extractable structure in sequential data for a computational model, while distillation covers any knowledge source for any consumer. The theoretical grounding covers a subset of the claimed operational space.

The opening uses "bounded context" (agent-specific in this KB) but the prior-work section cites human-reader fields (pedagogy, technical writing). The note oscillates between general and agent-specific definitions of distillation.
"Co-equal learning mechanism" and "main operation context engineering performs" are logically compatible but could create an impression that distillation is more central than constraining.
The source-to-distillate table is illustrative, not exhaustive -- no scope-claiming language, reasonable range of examples.
The "typically stays in natural language" qualifier leaves appropriate room for borderline medium cases.
The warning about behavioral influence loss accurately reflects the Faithful Self-Evolvers source with appropriate hedging.
The distillation-as-main-operation claim is well-aligned with the context engineering note.
The orthogonality claim and 2x2 matrix are perfectly mirrored in the constraining note.
The medium-constancy claim is strongly supported by the skills-derive-from-methodology note's detailed argument.
The description field accurately represents the note's central claims.

The core claim is that source tracking belongs at the source, not the target. The "Source-side tracking" section (the load-bearing section that explains the mechanism and the practical convention) is roughly equal in length to the opening paragraphs that motivate it. However, the "Two audiences, one link direction" section — a table that restates the same idea in a different format — adds little beyond what "Source-side tracking" already establishes. The note's real weight is in the convention ("Distilled into:" at the source footer) and the maintenance scenario it enables, but proportionally neither gets deeper treatment than the motivating setup.

The final sentence of the note ("Staleness detection flows in the direction of change: source changes -> maintainer sees downstream targets -> reviews them") restates the mechanism already described in "Source-side tracking" ("you're editing a methodology note, you see 'Distilled into: WRITING.md', you know to check whether WRITING.md needs updating"). The restatement is compact enough that it functions as a summary rather than dead weight, but it is doing the same work twice.

The note claims to be about distillation and source tracking within a knowledge base. All examples — WRITING.md, methodology notes, conventions, instructions — are native to this domain. No leaked framing from an unrelated source domain.
No formal notation or symbolic apparatus is present. The table in "Two audiences, one link direction" is presentational, not pseudo-formal.

The note presents a convention (the "Distilled into:" footer pattern) and a design rationale. Both are framed as design choices, not empirical findings. Assertions like "The dependency link belongs at the source, not the target" are stated directly, which is appropriate since this is a design note arguing for a convention, not reporting external evidence. The claim "The KB is small enough that grep is the query engine" is correctly scoped as a current-state observation.
No specific figures, data points, percentages, or named studies appear. All claims are about this KB's design.
No cross-domain evidence is cited. The note stays within its own domain (knowledge-base maintenance).

The note discusses artifacts and maintainers, not models. Language like "it doesn't know its sources" (in the table) is applied to the distilled artifact, not to an LLM, and reads as shorthand for "contains no record of its sources" — acceptable in context and unlikely to mislead.

The note claims "Without a record of what went into the distillation, there's no way to know which artifacts to review" (para 2). But the note itself later acknowledges a reverse lookup mechanism: "rg 'WRITING.md' kb/notes/ finds all notes linking to the target." If a maintainer knows which distilled artifact they care about, they can already find its sources via grep — even without "Distilled into:" links. The "no way to know" claim is about the forward direction (source changed, which targets are affected?), but the absolute phrasing oversells the gap. The reverse direction is cheap and always available; what's missing without source-side tracking is specifically the forward direction. The note knows this (the table and the grep sentence show it) but the paragraph 2 framing elides the distinction.

The note's framework presents exactly two options: links at the source ("Distilled into:") or links at the target (inline back-links in the distilled artifact). It argues for source-side and against target-side. But there is a third option the framework does not consider: a separate dependency manifest — a standalone file or section that maps sources to distilled targets, owned by neither. This is how many build systems work (Makefiles, package.json dependency lists). The linked note on make-like staleness detection describes precisely this kind of external dependency graph. A manifest would avoid both problems the note identifies (diluting distilled artifacts AND burdening source notes with forward pointers that may themselves go stale). The binary framing may still reach the right conclusion, but the space of alternatives is larger than presented.

The link to "indirection is costly in LLM instructions" is annotated as "motivates: why distilled artifacts shouldn't carry links back to sources." The indirection note argues that variables, config references, and abstraction layers cost interpretation overhead in LLM instructions. But a markdown link in a note is not the same kind of indirection — it does not require the LLM to resolve a variable or perform substitution. A link is inert unless followed. The indirection note's argument is about cognitive/computational overhead during execution, not about the mere presence of navigational metadata. The motivational relationship is plausible (both concern keeping distilled artifacts lean) but the mechanism is different from what the source note actually describes.

The link to "frontloading spares execution context" is annotated: "motivates: distillation is a form of frontloading; source-side tracking preserves the pre-frontloaded dependency structure." The frontloading note is about pre-computing static parts of instructions to spare execution context. The connection to source tracking is indirect: the note under review argues that distillation is a kind of frontloading (reasonable), and that source-side tracking preserves the dependency structure that frontloading eliminates from the target (also reasonable). But the frontloading note itself says nothing about tracking dependencies or provenance. The inference chain (frontloading removes derivation traces -> therefore we need source-side tracking to preserve them) is the reviewed note's own move, not something grounded in the source.

The note says distilled artifacts should have "no staleness signal" (table row), but the note also states that grep can find all sources linking to a distilled target. If sources carry "Distilled into:" links, and a maintainer runs grep on the distilled artifact's filename, the distilled artifact effectively does have an indirect staleness signal — it just requires the maintainer to initiate the query rather than seeing it inline. The table's clean "None" slightly overstates the asymmetry.

Claim 9 ("The KB is small enough that grep is the query engine") is presented as a supporting argument for the reverse-lookup being cheap. This implicitly scopes the entire design to small KBs. The note does not discuss what happens when the KB grows large enough that grep becomes expensive or produces too many false-positive matches. This is not necessarily a problem (the note may be intentionally scoped), but the scope limitation is implicit rather than stated.

The two-audience table is internally consistent with the rest of the note. The reader/links/staleness-signal decomposition faithfully maps to the prose arguments in each section. No definition drift detected — "distilled artifact," "source note," "staleness," and "focus" are used consistently throughout.

The link to "skills derive from methodology through distillation" is accurately attributed. That note establishes distillation as the methodology-to-skill derivation relationship, and the reviewed note builds on that foundation by addressing how to track the derivation. The "foundation" relationship annotation is correct.

The link to "link graph plus timestamps enables make-like staleness detection" is accurately attributed. That note describes using the link graph for staleness detection; the reviewed note's "Distilled into:" links provide additional dependency edges for that mechanism. The "extends" annotation is appropriate.

The note's title claim ("distilled artifacts need source tracking at the source") is faithfully developed by the body. The argument flows coherently: distilled artifacts should stay focused (para 1) -> but staleness detection needs dependency information (para 2) -> therefore track at the source (Source-side tracking section). No compressed summary elides tensions.

The note's central design recommendation (put "Distilled into:" links in source notes) was tested against boundary cases: (a) a distillation with a single source (the "typically draws from multiple" claim still works — a single source just has one entry); (b) a distilled artifact that is itself a source for further distillation (the pattern composes — the intermediate note carries both incoming links and its own "Distilled into:" footer); (c) a source that is distilled into many targets (the pattern scales linearly in footer entries). All boundary cases map cleanly.

The note states "A document has exactly one type" as a hard rule, and describes `structured-claim` as having a "promotion path" from `note` — "when a note's argument matures enough to fill Evidence/Reasoning sections, it earns the type." The word "earns" anthropomorphizes the document and smuggles in an evaluative judgment about maturity without stating the criteria. More importantly, the promotion path framing asserts a process convention as established fact, but it is this note's own construction. No source or ADR is cited for the promotion workflow.

The core claim of the note is the classification taxonomy itself (the base types table), which gets adequate treatment. However, `structured-claim` receives a full dedicated paragraph of explanation below the table while all other types (including `spec`, `review`, `adr`) get only their table-row descriptions. If `structured-claim` warrants expanded explanation, the other non-trivial types may too — or the `structured-claim` paragraph could be moved to its own type file, which already exists at `types/structured-claim.md` (linked from the table).

The migration table references old type names (`design`, `insight`, `analysis`, `research`, `comparison`) without context about where these old types came from or when they were in use. A reader encountering this note fresh has no frame for why this migration section exists. This is mild — the section is clearly labeled "Migration" — but the old names are residue from an earlier internal state of this KB that is not explained.
No formal notation is used. The tables are plain reference tables, not decorative formalism. Clean.
No specific figures, data points, or empirical claims appear. All references are to other notes in the KB, which are linked. Clean.
The note does not cite evidence from external domains. It is an internal specification. Clean.
The note is compact. No section re-explains what a prior section established. The opening sentence references two other notes for rationale and details, then proceeds directly to new content. Clean.

The word "earns" (noted under Confidence miscalibration) has a mild anthropomorphic quality, but the note is about documents and type systems, not models. No model-directed anthropomorphism is present. Clean.

The base types table lists seven types, but the note says "The `type` field is a free-form string" and that "directory-scoped `types/` folders document the structural expectations for each." This creates a tension: the table reads as an exhaustive enumeration ("The table below lists the common values") but the linked note directory-scoped-types-are-cheaper-than-global-types.md explicitly argues that types like `adr`, `review`, and `index` should be directory-local specializations, not global base types. A boundary case: a `source-review` type (mentioned in directory-scoped-types-are-cheaper-than-global-types.md under "Where affordances actually live") is absent from this table — is it a base type or a directory-scoped type? The note does not clarify the boundary between "common values" listed here and directory-scoped types documented elsewhere. This is a real gap: an agent reading only this spec cannot tell whether to add new types to this table or to a directory-scoped types/ folder.

The note claims that `note` "defines the shared fields (description, status, traits, areas)" but when checking types/note.md, the global fields listed there are: `description`, `type`, `traits`, `tags`, and `status`. The note says "areas" but the actual spec says "tags." This is a vocabulary mismatch — `areas` was likely the old name for what is now `tags`. The attribution does not match the source.

The migration table maps five old types but does not mention `spec`, `review`, or `index`, which appeared in the old flat enum listed in document-types-should-be-verifiable.md ("The original type system used a flat enum: design, analysis, insight, research, comparison, spec, review, index"). This implies `spec`, `review`, and `index` survived unchanged, but the note does not state this explicitly. A reader encountering one of these old types would not know whether migration is needed.

Boundary case: a document that is both a `spec` and an `adr` (e.g., an architecture decision that includes implementation-ready detail with Design/Implementation sections AND Context/Decision/Consequences sections). The note asserts "A document has exactly one type" but does not address how to resolve cases where a document satisfies the structural tests for multiple base types. The traits system handles combinatorial properties, but base type conflicts are not discussed.

Boundary case: a `text` file that contains YAML frontmatter but lacks a `description` field. The table says `note` requires "Has frontmatter with description" and `text` requires "No frontmatter." A file with frontmatter but no description falls between the two structural tests. Neither type covers it.

The note's type field says `type: spec`, and the description says "Taxonomy overview." The base types table defines `spec` as having "Design/Implementation sections," but this note has "Base types" and "Migration from old flat types" sections, not Design/Implementation. By the note's own structural test for `spec`, this note may not qualify as one. This is a minor self-referential inconsistency — the note classifies itself using a type whose structural test it does not satisfy.

The link to document-types-should-be-verifiable.md as "design rationale" is accurate. That note provides the full argument for why types should assert verifiable structural properties, and this note implements that design.
The link to types/note.md for "global fields, status ladder, traits, and design principles" is accurate. That spec does define all of those elements.

The description of `structured-claim` as extending `note` with Toulmin-derived sections aligns with claim-notes-should-use-toulmin-derived-sections-for-structured-argument.md, which develops the full rationale and explicitly maps to Toulmin components.

The migration table's treatment of `design` as "was subject matter, not structure" is grounded in document-types-should-be-verifiable.md, which argues at length that "design" dominated the KB without doing discriminatory work.
The migration of `insight` to "structured-claim (developed argument) or note (claim title, free-form body)" matches the promotion path described in the Toulmin note.

The seven base types form a coherent hierarchy: `text` (no frontmatter) at the bottom, `note` (frontmatter + description) as the base structured type, and five specializations with progressively more structural requirements. No pairwise contradictions found.

The migration table is consistent with the base types table — every "new encoding" in the migration table refers to a type or type+trait combination that exists in the base types table or the traits system defined in types/note.md.

The section "What went wrong with flat types" asserts "half the notes were 'design'" as a factual claim about the KB's historical state. This is presented as established fact but reads as an approximation or recollection — no data is cited. If this is a rough estimate, language like "a large fraction" or "many of the notes" would better match the epistemic status.

The core claim is in the title: document types should be verifiable. The section that carries the most weight for this claim — "What 'verifiable' means" — is the shortest substantive section (two short paragraphs). Meanwhile "Base types + traits" and "Programming language parallels" together receive significantly more development. The design solution (base types + traits) is important but is an application of the verifiability principle, not the principle itself. The note's center of gravity sits on the solution rather than the argument for the principle.

The note draws heavily on programming-language type theory (gradual typing, protocols, refinement types, soft typing). This is deliberate and well-framed — the "Programming language parallels" section explicitly maps each concept. However, the phrase "In object-oriented terms, this is like having `class ResearchInsight` but being forced to inherit from only one of `Research` or `Insight`" in the "What went wrong with flat types" section uses OO vocabulary mid-argument without flagging it as an analogy the way the parallels section does. It reads as though the author momentarily shifted registers.

The note uses "an agent reading `type: design` learns nothing" and similar formulations throughout. In context this is reasonable — the note explicitly defines its processor as an LLM agent and consistently uses "agent" as the term of art. The language is anthropomorphic but intentionally so and consistently applied. Flagging only for completeness; no action needed unless the author wants to tighten the distinction between agent-as-software and agent-as-reasoner.

The programming-language parallels are the note's explicit framing device, not leaked residue from a source. The note's claimed generality level (document type systems for agent-operated KBs) is maintained throughout; PL concepts are presented as analogies with clear mapping tables.

No formal notation, equations, or symbolic apparatus is used. The YAML blocks serve as concrete examples of the proposed system, not as pseudo-formal proofs. The tables are descriptive, not decorative.

No unsourced specific numbers, named studies, or empirical claims appear in the note. The "half the notes" claim (flagged under confidence miscalibration) is the closest case; all other claims are structural arguments or references to other notes in the KB.

The note's cross-domain moves (PL type theory to document classification) are explicitly bridged. Each parallel is mapped with a clear "X in PL corresponds to Y in this system because Z" structure. The "Programming language parallels" section does this systematically, and inline references (e.g., "like `Any` in a gradually typed language") are brief enough to work as illustrative shorthand.

Sections open with their own contributions. The "But our processor interprets underspecified instructions" section might appear to restate the previous section's point about enforcement, but it actually introduces a new concern (underspecification of type assignment itself) that the previous section did not address.

The base types table lists four types: `note`, `spec`, `review`, `index`. But the companion spec (document-classification.md) lists seven: `text`, `note`, `spec`, `review`, `index`, `adr`, `structured-claim`. The note itself later mentions `structured-claim` in the body ("What it called 'insight' becomes `structured-claim`...") but never adds it to the base types table. A reader relying on the table as the definitive enumeration would miss `text`, `adr`, and `structured-claim` as base types. Since the note presents the table as the solution to the flat-types problem ("Instead of a flat enum, use a base type plus traits"), this looks like an incomplete enumeration rather than a deliberate simplification.

The verifiability gradient (Section: The verifiability gradient) references a link to `deploy-time-learning-the-missing-middle.md` with the anchor text "verifiability gradient," suggesting the concept originates there. However, the deploy-time-learning note's verifiability gradient is about grades of artifact hardening (restructured prompts -> structured output schemas -> prompt tests/evals -> deterministic modules). The note's verifiability gradient is about document type maturation (untyped note -> traits accumulate -> base type promoted). These are different gradients applied to different domains. The note frames its gradient as continuous with the deploy-time-learning gradient ("This connects to the verifiability gradient"), but the mapping is loose: document type promotion does not correspond to any specific grade in the deploy-time-learning table. The link implies tighter alignment than the sources support.

The note claims "Type assignment is itself a case of storing an LLM output as constraining" and links to `storing-llm-outputs-is-constraining.md`. That source is about storing generated artifacts (code, documents, configuration) — the output of an LLM generation task. Type assignment is a classification decision, not a generation task; the "output" being stored is a label, not a document. The source's framework (resolving semantic underspecification + freezing against indeterminism) does apply, but the source develops its argument entirely around generated artifacts, not metadata labels. The note is extending the source's domain from "storing generated content" to "storing classification decisions," which is a reasonable inference but not directly grounded by the source.

The traits table lists three traits: `has-comparison`, `has-external-sources`, `has-implementation`. The note presents these as the trait system, but the document-classification spec may define additional traits. Boundary case: a document with external sources that also has a comparison section and implementation sketches — would three traits be sufficient to capture its properties? The note's own programming-language parallel to "protocols / structural typing" suggests traits should be open-ended ("A document satisfies `has-external-sources` if it references external material, regardless of whether someone labeled it"), but the table reads as a closed enumeration. A note clarifying that the trait list is extensible (or not) would resolve this ambiguity.

The programming language parallels section lists four PL concepts: gradual typing, protocols/structural typing, refinement types, soft typing. Boundary case: **sum types / union types** — the note's base types are effectively a sum type (a document is exactly one of note | spec | review | index), yet this parallel goes unmentioned while the more exotic "refinement types" is included. This is a minor gap — the note is illustrating, not enumerating — but the omission of the most obvious parallel is notable.

The note says "`note` is the base type that makes no structural claim — like `Any` in a gradually typed language." But earlier, the "What verifiable means" section argues that types should "assert a structural property you can check" and that types failing this test are useless. If `note` makes no structural claim, it fails the note's own verifiability test. The note doesn't explicitly acknowledge this tension. The gradual-typing analogy suggests this is intentional (you need `Any` to bootstrap the system), but a reader could see `note` as contradicting the title claim. The tolerance-of-misclassification section partially addresses this by framing types as optimization rather than correctness, but the tension between "types should be verifiable" and "the default type is unverifiable" is never directly stated.

The note says types help agents make "informed rather than blind" navigation decisions and links to `agents-navigate-by-deciding-what-to-read-next.md`. That source discusses pointers (links, index entries, search results, skill descriptions) and the context they carry. It never mentions document types or the `type` frontmatter field as a navigation mechanism. The note's inference — that types serve the same role as context-carrying pointers — is reasonable but is the note's own contribution, not something the source argues.

The note claims "our processor is an LLM that interprets underspecified instructions" and links to `agentic-systems-interpret-underspecified-instructions.md`. That source directly supports this: it defines semantic underspecification of natural language specs as the deeper difference from traditional programming and develops the spec-to-program projection model. The vocabulary and framing align precisely.

The note claims the text testing pyramid "sketches what enforcement could look like" and links to `automated-tests-for-text.md`. That source directly describes deterministic checks for structural contracts and LLM rubrics for judgment-dependent traits, and explicitly says "types define what to check, tests do the checking." The attribution is accurate.

The note claims "type assignment is itself a case of storing an LLM output as constraining" and links to `storing-llm-outputs-is-constraining.md`. While the domain extension is noted as a WARN above, the core mechanism described in that source — resolving semantic underspecification by committing to one interpretation — does genuinely apply to type assignment. The logical structure of the inference is sound even if the source's examples don't cover this case.

The note's argument flows coherently from problem (flat types are unverifiable and force false choices) through solution (base types + traits) to qualification (tolerance of misclassification). No pairwise contradictions between sections beyond the `note`-as-`Any` tension flagged as INFO.

The migration table in "Base types + traits" is consistent with the problems diagnosed in "What went wrong with flat types": `design` maps to `note` (subject matter, not structure), `research` maps to `note` + `has-external-sources`, `insight` maps to `structured-claim` or `note`. The solutions directly address the diagnosed problems.

The notation `||P||_t ≤ M` appears twice (body and open questions as `||·||_t`) but does no formal work. The variables are not defined (what is P? what is M? what does the subscript t range over?), no assumptions are stated, and no quantitative prediction or derivation follows from the expression. The surrounding prose — "the cost norm depends on what you're asking the model to do" — carries the full argument without the notation. Deleting the formula and writing "the effective cost of a prompt depends on the task" would lose nothing.

The phrase "In the interpretation developed here, they are two dimensions along which prompts consume bounded effective budget" presents the note's own synthesis as a discovered fact rather than a proposed framing. The note's caveats section correctly hedges ("The claim should stay qualitative"), but the main body uses unhedged language for what is the note's own interpretive construction, not a finding from either source.

The note is pitched at a general level (effective context as a property of model-task pairs), and most of its language stays there. However, the phrase "recursive steps get focused local frames" in the ConvexBench paragraph is fairly domain-specific to symbolic/mathematical reasoning without being flagged as such. A reader unfamiliar with ConvexBench may not know what "recursive steps" or "focused local frames" mean concretely. This is mild — the note does name ConvexBench — but the phrasing reads more like a summary of the paper's mechanism than like a claim at the note's generality level.

The core synthesis — that effective context is relational and should be treated as a task-shaped cost measure — is compressed into a single paragraph (the one beginning "The synthesis is"). The two source summaries each get their own paragraph of comparable length. The most original contribution of the note (the relational framing and its connection to the bounded-context orchestration model) is thinner than the setup. This is not severe because the note is concise overall, but the balance slightly underweights the note's own contribution.

All specific claims are attributed. The "5,331 tokens" figure is traced to ConvexBench with a citation. The "11 frontier models" figure is traced to Paulsen. No floating numbers or uncited empirical claims.
Both sources are from the LLM evaluation domain, and the note's claims are about LLMs. No cross-domain transfer is attempted. The evidence and the claims live in the same domain.
The note is compact (four substantive paragraphs plus caveats and open questions). No section reopens by restating a prior section's conclusion. Each paragraph advances a distinct point.
The note uses "handle," "fail," and "use" for model behavior, which are standard operational language. No verbs implying mental states ("understands," "believes," "knows") appear.

The note says Paulsen "measures Maximum Effective Context Window (MECW) across 11 frontier models and finds it far smaller than advertised limits. Crucially, the threshold shifts by problem type." The Paulsen source does support this, but the note then uses this as evidence that "effective context is relational" in a way that includes prompt difficulty / compositional complexity as a dimension. Paulsen's tasks (needle-in-a-haystack, counting, sorting, summarization) vary by task *type* but do not isolate compositional complexity as a separate axis. The note's caveats paragraph acknowledges this ("Paulsen does not cleanly isolate pure volume from task difficulty"), but the body paragraphs present Paulsen and ConvexBench as two independent legs of a convergent argument where Paulsen covers the task-type axis and ConvexBench covers the complexity axis. In practice, Paulsen's finding is that MECW varies by task type, not that it varies by complexity independent of task type. The note's synthesis — that effective context is jointly determined by task type *and* complexity — goes beyond what the two sources independently demonstrate, because neither source measures the interaction between the two dimensions. The convergence is weaker than "two independent sources converge on this" suggests: each source covers a different axis, but no source covers the joint claim.

The note claims "effective context is relational: model choice matters, task type matters, and prompt difficulty changes the effective cost of a prompt." This enumeration of factors (model, task type, prompt difficulty) omits several factors that both sources hint at. Paulsen's source discusses **position effects** (retrieval drops from 76% to 66% when key info moves from position 1 to position 2), **ratio of relevant to total tokens** (up to 25% impact), and **training-time sequence length** as distinct factors affecting MECW. The note's caveats mention "position, framing, and scope contamination" but fold them into "prompt difficulty" as a catch-all. This is a defensible modeling choice, but a reader might ask: does "prompt difficulty" become so broad that it loses predictive content? If position effects, framing quality, relevant-to-irrelevant ratio, and compositional depth all collapse into "difficulty," the relational claim becomes near-tautological — effective context depends on everything about the prompt except its token count. The note could be clearer about whether "prompt difficulty" is a placeholder for multiple separable dimensions or a genuinely unified concept.

The note says ConvexBench shows "performance collapsing with compositional depth at just 5,331 tokens." The ConvexBench source says 5,331 tokens is the count at depth 100. The source describes performance degrading "beginning as early as depth 5" and reaching F1 of approximately 0.2 at depth 100. The note's phrasing "at just 5,331 tokens" could be read as implying collapse happens at that specific token count, when actually the collapse is progressive across depths 5-100 and the 5,331 figure is the token count at the extreme end. The attribution is not wrong, but it compresses the progression into what reads like a point event.

Consider a trivial lookup task: "What is the capital of France?" in a context containing only that question. Effective context here is essentially model-independent and task-type-independent — every model succeeds, the task type is irrelevant, and difficulty is negligible. The note's framework handles this fine (the cost norm is very small, well under any threshold), but this simplest case reveals that the "relational" property only becomes interesting above some complexity floor. The note never identifies when the relational property starts to bite, which could help scope its practical applicability.

check: Completeness / boundary case — between enumerated items
finding: The note draws a clean two-leg argument: Paulsen covers task type, ConvexBench covers complexity. But consider a case that sits between these: a long-context summarization task (Paulsen's domain) where the material has deeply nested argumentative structure (ConvexBench's domain). Does the note's framework predict what happens? The ||P||_t formalism suggests yes (the cost norm would be high on both dimensions), but neither source provides evidence for this joint case. This is an untested interior of the claimed space — not a flaw, but a gap the note could acknowledge more explicitly.

The note uses "complexity" in two related but distinguishable senses. In the opening paragraph: "one compositionally shallow and cleanly framed, the other requiring deep structured reasoning or burying the relevant information in a harder-to-use presentation" — here complexity includes both compositional depth and poor framing/presentation. In the ConvexBench paragraph, complexity is specifically compositional depth (nested function composition). Later, when connecting to the context-efficiency note's "two-axis model," complexity appears as the general "how hard the tokens are to use" dimension. These are compatible but not identical — compositional depth is one contributor to difficulty, not the whole of it. The note's caveats treat framing as part of "prompt difficulty," which partially resolves this, but the body switches between the narrow (ConvexBench-style compositional depth) and broad (general difficulty) senses without flagging the shift.

The note claims its interpretation "is weaker and cleaner than treating MECW as a single parameterized scalar MECW(model, task_type, complexity)." The claim that relational framing is "weaker" than parametric framing is presented as self-evident, but it depends on what "weaker" means. A relational claim (X depends on Y and Z) is weaker in commitment than a parametric claim (X = f(Y, Z)) only if we mean "makes fewer quantitative predictions." But a relational claim that folds everything into a general cost norm ||P||_t without specifying what t captures could be criticized as weaker in a less flattering sense — it may be less falsifiable. The note does not address this tension.

The note's claim that Paulsen finds MECW "far smaller than advertised limits" and that "the threshold shifts by problem type" is well-supported. Paulsen's abstract states "MECW is, not only, drastically different from the MCW but also shifts based on the problem type." The attribution is accurate.

The note's claim that ConvexBench shows "performance collapsing with compositional depth" and "recovering when recursive steps get focused local frames" matches the source. ConvexBench reports F1 dropping from 1.0 to approximately 0.2, and the agentic framework with focused context achieving F1=1.0 at all depths. The recovery claim is directly supported.

The note claims it "interprets that relationship more naturally as a task-shaped cost measure ||P||_t <= M." The bounded-context orchestration model note explicitly defines ||P||_t as "the effective cost of prompt P for call type t" and states that its "definition depends on the kind of work the call is doing," citing this note as the empirical basis. The cross-reference is bidirectional and consistent.

The note claims to sharpen "the context-efficiency note's two-axis model." The context-efficiency note explicitly describes "volume (how many tokens) and complexity (how hard they are to use)" as two dimensions and links back to this note with "sharpens: makes explicit why usable context cannot be treated as a single per-model number." The relationship is accurate and reciprocal.

The caveats section ("The evidence is convergent, not final") honestly acknowledges limitations that the body presents more confidently. The caveats note that Paulsen confounds volume with task difficulty and that ConvexBench does not measure a joint surface. These qualifications are consistent with the body's claims — the body does not overclaim beyond what the caveats retract. The final sentence ("The claim should stay qualitative") appropriately scopes the note's ambition.

check: Internal consistency — open questions vs claims
finding: The open questions section asks "Is there a clean empirical regime where volume can be varied while task difficulty and compositional complexity stay mostly fixed?" — this is consistent with the caveats' acknowledgment that neither source cleanly isolates a single dimension. The questions do not contradict the body; they identify the natural next steps.

The four strategies are the note's own construction but are presented with assertive framing: "Strategies ordered by expertise required" as a section heading, numbered 1–4 as if the ordering is established, and "Activation is near-perfect" (strategy 1), "Activation is weaker than with targeted probes but substantially better than with undirected review" (strategy 4). These comparative activation claims are not grounded in cited evidence — they are plausible but proposed. The ordering is stated as fact rather than flagged as a proposed framework.

"In human code review, McConnell's *Code Complete* found that perspective-assigned reviewers uncover more defects than undirected review — the same principle applies to LLMs, where the role assignment shapes which stored knowledge gets activated." The bridge ("the same principle applies to LLMs") is asserted but not argued. McConnell's finding is about human cognition under review conditions. The note needs to state *why* the mechanism transfers — what shared property of human reviewers and LLMs makes perspective assignment effective in both? The parenthetical foundation note establishes that LLMs and humans share a cue-based activation structure, but this note does not invoke that bridge.

The note's title and opening claim generality — "elicitation" and "question-generation systems" — is domain-neutral. But the body leans heavily on software engineering examples: "on-call engineer at 2AM," "1,000 concurrent users," "network-calling code, address thundering herd, connection exhaustion, timeout propagation," "100x scale," "two instances of this run simultaneously," "runs for a year." The non-software examples ("opposing counsel," "contract review, address jurisdiction conflicts, liability caps, termination triggers," "end user who will encounter this at scale") exist but are outnumbered. The framing is closer to "elicitation for code review" than the title promises.

The core claim (title) is that elicitation requires *maintained* question-generation systems. The "Checklist lifecycle" section (which carries the "maintained" part of the claim) gets roughly 90 words across 5 bullet points. The "Strategies ordered by expertise required" section (which is about elicitation techniques, not maintenance) gets roughly 350 words. The maintenance argument — the thing that distinguishes this note from a simple strategies catalog — is underdeveloped relative to the strategies it depends on. The closing sentence ("Without it, checklists decay: they cover yesterday's failures while tomorrow's slip through") carries weight but arrives after a thin enumeration.

"the model activates on its own" (in "Composing strategies" section) attributes self-directed initiative to the model. The note is generally careful about this (it uses "surfaces," "activated," "stored"), but "activates on its own" is a mild anthropomorphism. Similarly, "the model systematically misses" attributes systematic oversight rather than describing an output pattern.
No formal notation or mathematical apparatus present. The note uses prose throughout, with numbered lists that serve organizational rather than formal-logical functions. Clean.

All specific claims are either self-contained (framework is the note's own) or cited. The McConnell reference is attributed. The metric definitions in "Measurement" are self-documenting. No unattributed empirical claims found.

Section openings advance the argument rather than restating prior conclusions. The opening paragraph of "Beyond known failure families" transitions cleanly from known to unknown unknowns. "Composing strategies" does not re-explain the individual strategies. Clean.

The four-strategy ordering claims to be ordered by "expertise required of the user," but the boundary between strategies 2 (perspective assignments) and 4 (structured adversarial prompts) is blurry. "What happens with 1,000 concurrent users?" is listed as an example of perspective assignment (strategy 2), but it fits equally well as a structured adversarial prompt (strategy 4) — it is a parameterized template ("what happens at Nx scale?") that does not require domain-specific knowledge. The note itself uses this example under strategy 2, yet the structure of the question matches strategy 4's definition. If the same concrete probe can be classified as either strategy 2 or strategy 4, the taxonomy has an overlap that the ordering does not acknowledge.

The note says "McConnell's Code Complete found that perspective-assigned reviewers uncover more defects than undirected review — the same principle applies to LLMs, where the role assignment shapes which stored knowledge gets activated." The source (the-bug-that-shipped) references McConnell but attributes a narrower claim: "assigning reviewers specific perspectives... can uncover more defects than general review." The note's extension to LLMs ("the same principle applies") is the note's own inference, not McConnell's or the source's finding. The source demonstrates that targeted questions get better answers than open review, but it does not test perspective assignment as a distinct mechanism — it tests direct targeted probes ("What happens if 1,000 clients hit this simultaneously?"). The note conflates the source's evidence for strategy 1 (direct targeted probes) with support for strategy 2 (perspective assignments).

The five-step checklist lifecycle (seed, enforce, log misses, distill updates, prune stale probes) is presented as a complete maintenance loop. A boundary case: what about re-prioritization or re-ordering of probes? As the set grows, the context budget becomes a constraint — not every probe can be included in every review. The lifecycle includes "prune stale probes" but does not address selection or triage among active probes when the set exceeds what fits in a single pass. This is a missing step between enforce and prune that matters at scale.

The four strategies are implicitly scoped to probe-based elicitation — all four involve formulating questions or perspectives. A boundary case: environmental or architectural elicitation, where the workflow structure itself activates knowledge (e.g., requiring the model to produce a deployment checklist before marking a task complete, or routing code through a second model with a different system prompt). These are not probes in the sense the note describes — they are structural constraints that force activation. The note's "two-pass review" section gestures toward this but does not name it as a distinct strategy class. This may be intentional scoping rather than a gap, but it is worth flagging because the title claim ("elicitation requires maintained question-generation systems") implies question generation is the only mechanism, when structural forcing is an alternative.

The link to professional-software-developers-dont-vibe-they-control is described as "complement: expert practitioners compensate for activation limits through explicit planning, narrow tasking, and supervision — a human version of two-pass review."
